title: Intraoperative Myocardial Ischemia
summary: A 68-year-old man with coronary disease undergoing open lower-extremity revascularization develops demand ischemia when light anesthesia and blood loss drive tachycardia and hypertension. Learners must read the ST-segment change and new ectopy, restore the myocardial oxygen supply-demand balance with oxygen, analgesia/depth, rate control, and coronary perfusion pressure, use nitroglycerin without dropping the pressure too far, and be ready for the ventricular fibrillation that follows if ischemia is left to run.
learningObjectives: Recognize intraoperative myocardial ischemia from new ST-segment depression, T-wave change, or ischemic ectopy against the clinical backdrop of tachycardia, hypertension, hypotension, or anemia.

Reason explicitly in supply-versus-demand terms: reduce demand (heart rate, contractility, wall tension) while protecting supply (oxygenation, hemoglobin, coronary perfusion pressure, diastolic time).

Treat in the right order for the hemodynamic picture: 100% oxygen and adequate depth/analgesia first, then rate control for tachycardia, a vasopressor to defend coronary perfusion pressure, and nitroglycerin only when the pressure allows.

Know the verified doses: esmolol 0.5 mg/kg IV over 1 minute then 50-300 mcg/kg/min, IV nitroglycerin started at 5-10 mcg/min and titrated, phenylephrine 100 mcg boluses or a norepinephrine infusion, and aspirin 162-325 mg when appropriate.
Manage the hypotensive ischemia trap: nitroglycerin and beta-blockade both lower an already-low pressure, so restore perfusion pressure and treat anemia before nitrates.

Escalate to the ACLS shockable-arrest pathway without delay if ischemia degenerates into ventricular fibrillation, and plan the postoperative work-up (12-lead ECG, troponin, cardiology) and disposition.
setup: OR mid-case for an open femoral-popliteal bypass in a vasculopath: general endotracheal anesthesia established, moderate ongoing surgical blood loss, arterial line transducing.
This is an arterial-line case: the monitor shows live beat-to-beat pressure (set monitoring.artLine if you prefer, or narrate cuff readings).
The simulated monitor does not draw ST segments — faculty must verbally report the ECG: 'lead II and V5 now show 2 mm of new horizontal ST depression', and later 'multiform PVCs appearing'.
Labeled props: esmolol, metoprolol, phenylephrine 100 mcg/mL, norepinephrine infusion, nitroglycerin infusion, aspirin 325 mg, heparin, and a defibrillator with pads on the patient.
Confederate circulator/surgeon who can quantify blood loss ('another 400 mL in the field') and pause when asked.
Preop note: prior drug-eluting stent to the RCA two years ago, still on aspirin; exertional angina climbing stairs; last echo EF 45%.

patient.name: Walter Bishop
medications: Metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily (held this morning)
pmh: Coronary artery disease with drug-eluting stent to the right coronary artery 2 years ago
Exertional angina (2 flights of stairs); last transthoracic echo ejection fraction 45%
Peripheral arterial disease with rest pain, for femoral-popliteal bypass
Type 2 diabetes and hypertension; former smoker, 40 pack-years

airway.notes: Already intubated with a 8.0 ETT at 22 cm; grade 1 view at induction.

patient.plan: Open femoral-popliteal bypass under general endotracheal anesthesia with an arterial line; goal is a controlled heart rate and a coronary-protective blood pressure throughout.

phases.label: Stable Maintenance
phases.description: Controlled anesthetic, rate and pressure near baseline in a patient with coronary disease.

phases.label: Provocation
phases.description: Light anesthesia and blood loss drive tachycardia and hypertension — rising myocardial oxygen demand.

phases.label: Ischemia
phases.description: New ST-segment depression and ectopy: demand has outstripped supply.

phases.label: Treatment
phases.description: Restore the supply-demand balance: oxygen, depth, rate control, perfusion pressure, nitroglycerin.

phases.label: Arrest (if unmanaged)
phases.description: Ischemia degenerates into ventricular fibrillation — shockable-arrest pathway.

phases.label: Resolution
phases.description: ST segments recover; plan the work-up and disposition.

events.label: Blood loss under light anesthesia
events.description: Ongoing surgical bleeding and a light plane leave the patient tachycardic and hypertensive — the exact combination that raises myocardial oxygen demand while shortening the diastolic coronary filling time.

events.label: New ST-segment depression
events.description: Faculty report: 'lead II and V5 now show 2 mm of new horizontal ST depression.' Demand ischemia has begun; heart rate stays high and the pressure is strained.

events.label: Ischemic ectopy and pump strain
events.description: Multiform PVCs appear and the pressure begins to sag as the ischemic ventricle contracts poorly (faculty: 'frequent multiform PVCs, ST depression now 3 mm'). This is the window before ventricular fibrillation.

events.label: Ventricular fibrillation arrest
events.description: Unrelieved ischemia degenerates into VF: the arterial trace collapses to a flat, pulseless waveform and EtCO2 falls. Fire only if ischemia is left unmanaged. Start the ACLS shockable-arrest pathway — defibrillate.

events.label: Defibrillation and ROSC
events.description: High-quality CPR, a 200 J biphasic shock, epinephrine 1 mg, and amiodarone 300 mg restore an organized rhythm and a pulse (faculty: 'sinus rhythm, ST changes improving'). Fire after the team defibrillates.

events.label: Depth and analgesia restored
events.description: Deepening the anesthetic and giving opioid blunts the sympathetic drive: heart rate and pressure ease toward less ischemic territory.

events.label: Esmolol rate control
events.description: Esmolol 0.5 mg/kg IV over 1 minute, then a 50-300 mcg/kg/min infusion, brings the rate down and lengthens diastolic coronary filling — the single most effective demand-reducing move when the pressure tolerates it.

events.label: Coronary perfusion pressure defended
events.description: Phenylephrine 100 mcg boluses (or a norepinephrine infusion) restores diastolic and mean pressure, protecting coronary supply — the prerequisite before any nitroglycerin in a patient whose pressure has fallen.

events.label: Nitroglycerin infusion
events.description: With an adequate pressure restored, IV nitroglycerin started at 5-10 mcg/min and titrated dilates the coronaries and drops wall tension; the ST depression improves. Watch the pressure — nitrates can undo the perfusion you just defended.

events.label: Ischemia resolves
events.description: Faculty: 'ST segments back to baseline, ectopy gone.' With demand controlled and supply protected, the ECG normalizes. The team turns to the 12-lead, troponin, and disposition.

expectedActions.label: Recognize myocardial ischemia from the new ST-segment depression and ectopy
State the supply-versus-demand problem: high rate and pressure with ongoing blood loss
expectedActions.description: Naming the mechanism drives the correct order of treatment.

expectedActions.label: Ensure 100% oxygen and adequate anesthetic depth/analgesia to cut sympathetic demand
Control the heart rate (esmolol 0.5 mg/kg IV then 50-300 mcg/kg/min) once the pressure tolerates it
Defend coronary perfusion pressure with a vasopressor (phenylephrine 100 mcg or norepinephrine) and treat anemia/blood loss
Give nitroglycerin (start 5-10 mcg/min IV, titrate) only once the blood pressure allows
expectedActions.description: Recognize the hypotensive-ischemia trap: nitrates and beta-blockers both lower an already-low pressure.

expectedActions.label: Call for help, alert the surgeon, and ask them to pause stimulation and control bleeding
If VF occurs, start CPR and defibrillate immediately (200 J biphasic), epinephrine 1 mg, amiodarone 300 mg
Obtain a 12-lead ECG and troponin, give aspirin 162-325 mg if appropriate, and consult cardiology
Plan disposition: ICU monitoring, continued rate/pressure control, and a joint decision to proceed or abort

expectedProgression: The case is stable until light anesthesia and ongoing blood loss drive the heart rate over 100 with hypertension — rising myocardial oxygen demand in a patient with fixed coronary supply.
Around 5 minutes new ST-segment depression appears (faculty-reported); the diagnosis is demand ischemia, not a primary rhythm problem.
Learners should go to 100% oxygen, deepen anesthesia and add analgesia, ask the surgeon to pause and control bleeding, and start rate control while defending the pressure.
If ischemia is untreated, ectopy and pump strain appear and the pressure falls — the hypotensive-ischemia trap where nitrates and beta-blockers are dangerous until perfusion pressure is restored.
Unmanaged, the rhythm degenerates into ventricular fibrillation; the team must pivot instantly to the ACLS shockable-arrest pathway and defibrillate.
With demand controlled (depth, rate) and supply protected (pressure, hemoglobin, then nitroglycerin), the ST segments recover.
The team obtains a 12-lead and troponin, gives aspirin if appropriate, involves cardiology, and plans ICU disposition and whether to continue surgery.
correctManagement: Increase FiO2 to 100% and confirm oxygenation and hemoglobin are adequate — protect myocardial oxygen supply first.
Deepen the anesthetic and treat pain to remove the sympathetic drive behind the tachycardia and hypertension.
Ask the surgeon to pause stimulation and control bleeding; call for help and the code cart early.
Reduce demand with rate control: esmolol 0.5 mg/kg IV over 1 minute, then a 50-300 mcg/kg/min infusion (or titrated metoprolol), provided the pressure tolerates it.
Defend coronary perfusion pressure with phenylephrine 100 mcg boluses or a norepinephrine infusion, and replace blood loss — diastolic pressure and hemoglobin are supply.
Once the pressure is adequate, start IV nitroglycerin at 5-10 mcg/min and titrate to relieve ischemia, watching for hypotension; avoid nitrates while the patient is hypotensive.
Give aspirin 162-325 mg when the surgical field allows and there is no contraindication.

If ventricular fibrillation develops, start high-quality CPR and defibrillate immediately (200 J biphasic / per device), epinephrine 1 mg IV every 3-5 minutes, amiodarone 300 mg then 150 mg for refractory VF.
After stabilization obtain a 12-lead ECG and troponin, involve cardiology, and plan ICU monitoring and the intraoperative decision to proceed or abort.
commonErrors: Treating the number, not the mechanism: pushing nitroglycerin or a beta-blocker into a falling pressure and precipitating hypotensive ischemia or arrest.
Leaving the anesthetic light and the patient in pain while reaching for cardiac drugs — the sympathetic drive is the engine of the ischemia.
Ignoring ongoing blood loss and anemia, which starve myocardial oxygen supply no matter what drugs are given.
Chasing the tachycardia with a beta-blocker before restoring perfusion pressure in a hypotensive patient.
Missing new ectopy and ST changes because the simulated monitor doesn't draw ST segments — the faculty cue must be acted on.
Delaying defibrillation when ischemia degenerates into VF, or forgetting this is now a shockable-arrest algorithm.
Forgetting the after-care: no 12-lead, no troponin, no cardiology, and a routine PACU disposition for a patient who had intraoperative ischemia.

points: Intraoperative myocardial ischemia is usually a supply-demand imbalance, not a coronary occlusion: tachycardia, hypertension, hypotension, anemia, and light anesthesia are the common drivers, and each is treatable.

The ECG is the intraoperative monitor for ischemia — new horizontal or downsloping ST depression, T-wave change, or fresh ectopy in a lead II/V5 configuration. Five-lead monitoring with V5 markedly improves sensitivity.

Order matters and depends on the pressure: oxygen, depth, and analgesia always; rate control and nitroglycerin only when the pressure tolerates them; a vasopressor to defend coronary perfusion pressure when it doesn't.
Verified doses: esmolol 0.5 mg/kg IV over 1 minute then 50-300 mcg/kg/min; IV nitroglycerin 5-10 mcg/min titrated; phenylephrine 100 mcg boluses or norepinephrine infusion; aspirin 162-325 mg.
The hypotensive-ischemia trap is the teaching point: both nitroglycerin and beta-blockade lower an already-low pressure, so restore perfusion pressure and hemoglobin first.

Ischemia can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation; recognizing the transition and defibrillating without delay (200 J biphasic, epinephrine 1 mg q3-5 min, amiodarone 300 mg) is the same reflex as any shockable arrest.
After-care defines quality: 12-lead ECG, serial troponin, aspirin, cardiology involvement, ICU monitoring, and an explicit proceed-versus-abort decision with the surgeon.
questions: What was the first sign of ischemia, and how did you separate demand ischemia from a primary arrhythmia?
Walk through your supply-versus-demand reasoning — what were you doing to each side of the balance?
Why is the order of oxygen/depth, then rate control, then nitroglycerin important, and how did the blood pressure change it?
The pressure fell as the ischemia worsened — why is that the most dangerous moment for nitroglycerin and beta-blockers?
What esmolol and nitroglycerin doses did you choose, and how did you titrate them?
When it became VF, how quickly did you pivot to the shockable-arrest algorithm, and what did you do first?
What does this patient need after the ST segments recover, and what do you tell the surgeon and the next team?
